summary: The post argues that engineers and managers often make the mistake of focusing on technical skill improvement when they should be developing leadership capabilities. While early-career engineers should focus on coding and technical skills, senior engineers and managers need to shift toward becoming 'multipliers' who enhance their teams through teaching, process improvement, and strategic thinking. Managers who cling to coding are often bottlenecks, and the real growth path is stepping back to observe and fix problems at a macro level. The key transition is from additive individual contribution to multiplicative team impact.
key_points: Junior engineers add value by doing tasks; senior engineers and managers add value by multiplying their team's effectiveness
Very few engineers write code of such volume and complexity that coding alone enhances the entire organization
Managers who insist on writing lots of code often become overworked bottlenecks rather than effective leaders
The solution isn't for managers to carve out coding time, but to not become a manager until coding is second nature
Growing into leadership requires getting out of your comfort zone and focusing on what makes you a multiplier
money_quotes: What got us here won't get us there.
After a certain point, it is more important to focus on what will make you a multiplier on the team.
Very very few engineers write code of such volume and complexity that simply by writing code they enhance the entire organization.
Managers that stubbornly hold onto the idea that they must write a lot of code are often either overworked, bottlenecks, or both.
Don't get pushed into management until you've spent enough time coding that it is second nature.
If you think that writing more code is the unlock for you to manage your team better, to grow as a better leader, you're going backwards.
You're not going to code your team out of crunch mode by yourself, so spend your time preventing them from getting into crunch mode in the first place.
key_insight: The transition from individual contributor to leader requires shifting focus from personal technical output to becoming a multiplier who enhances the entire team's effectiveness.
